title: Why Money is Freedom: Essential Wealth Strategies Every Woman Needs to Know
guestBio: Melissa Northway is a Financial Educator and Wealth Strategist with True Path Financial and former President of NABO California. She's passionate about bringing high-net-worth financial strategies to everyday women and families, helping them protect their assets, reduce taxes, and build generational wealth.
excerpt: Financial strategist Melissa Northway reveals why women disproportionately end up poor in retirement and shares the wealth-building secrets typically reserved for high-net-worth individuals. This episode will transform how you think about money as a tool for freedom.
metaDescription: Learn wealth strategies for women from financial expert Melissa Northway on cash value life insurance, tax planning & financial freedom.
keyTakeaways: Money equals freedom—the freedom to live life without financial worry and make choices based on values, not limitations.
Women need to take control of their financial planning instead of leaving it to their partners, as statistics show elderly women are disproportionately poor.
Your greatest asset is yourself—protect your earning potential with proper insurance and living benefit riders that provide access to death benefits while alive.
Cash value life insurance functions as a Tier 1 asset with a zero floor, meaning you can't lose money while building tax-advantaged wealth.
Asset swap strategies used by billionaires like Zuckerberg can be adapted for everyday women to optimize tax efficiency.
Developing AI skills can increase income by 10-25%, making it crucial for women to embrace technology for financial advancement.
Approaching money with a 'beginner's mind' and being coachable allows you to learn strategies that can transform your financial future.

pullQuotes.text: Money is freedom—freedom to live the life you want without financial worry

timestamps.label: Why Women Don't Talk About Money
The Statistics: Why Women End Up Poor

content:
What if everything you've been told about money is wrong? What if the key to your freedom isn't working harder, saving every penny, or cutting back on small luxuries—but instead understanding money as a tool for liberation?

In this transformative episode of The Manage Her Podcast, host Aimee Rickabus sits down with financial strategist Melissa Northway to shatter conventional wisdom about women and wealth. Melissa brings a refreshing perspective: money isn't the root of all evil or something to feel guilty about accumulating. Instead, money is freedom—the freedom to live life on your terms, make choices based on your values rather than your bank account, and create the impact you're meant to have in this world.

The Uncomfortable Truth About Women and Money

Melissa doesn't sugarcoat the reality: women are disproportionately poor in their later years, and it's not because they're bad with money. The statistics are sobering, but they're also a wake-up call. Too many women have abdicated financial responsibility to their partners, assuming someone else will take care of their future security. This mindset isn't just dangerous—it's disempowering.

The conversation reveals why women don't talk about money the way men do. While men discuss investments and strategies on the golf course, women often feel uncomfortable even mentioning their financial goals. This silence perpetuates a cycle of financial illiteracy that keeps women dependent and vulnerable. It's time to break that cycle.

Melissa emphasizes that changing your relationship with money starts with changing your mindset. You have permission to want wealth. You have permission to talk about money openly. You have permission to learn strategies that have traditionally been reserved for the wealthy elite. Your financial education is not selfish—it's essential.

Beyond Budgeting: Real Wealth-Building Strategies

This episode goes far beyond typical financial advice about budgeting and expense tracking. Melissa introduces concepts that might sound foreign but are incredibly powerful: cash value life insurance, living benefit riders, asset swaps, and tax-advantaged wealth accumulation strategies. These aren't just for millionaires—they're tools that everyday women can and should understand and utilize.

Cash value life insurance, for instance, functions as what Melissa calls a "Tier 1 asset" with a zero floor. This means your money can grow but never decrease, providing both protection and wealth accumulation in one vehicle. It's a concept that challenges traditional thinking about life insurance as merely a death benefit and reframes it as a living financial tool.

The discussion of living benefit riders is particularly compelling. These riders allow you to access your death benefit while you're still alive if you become critically ill or injured. Melissa shares real stories of clients who have utilized these benefits, transforming what could have been financial catastrophes into manageable situations with dignity intact.

Protecting Your Greatest Asset

One of the most powerful concepts Melissa presents is the idea that you are your greatest asset. Your ability to earn income, to create value, to contribute to your family and community—this is what needs protection above all else. Yet most people spend more time insuring their cars than protecting their earning potential.

This perspective shift is crucial for women, who often prioritize everyone else's needs before their own financial security. Melissa gives you permission to invest in protecting yourself, to see personal financial protection not as selfish but as responsible stewardship of your family's future.

The asset swap strategies she discusses—the same techniques used by billionaires like Mark Zuckerberg when selling Facebook stock—demonstrate how sophisticated financial planning can optimize tax efficiency and wealth preservation. These aren't mysterious secrets reserved for the ultra-wealthy; they're learnable strategies that can benefit anyone willing to understand them.

The Future of Women and Wealth

Melissa's work extends beyond traditional financial planning. She's co-founding the National Association for Women in AI (NAWAI), recognizing that developing AI skills can increase income by 10-25%. This forward-thinking approach acknowledges that financial freedom in the future will require embracing technological advancement, not fearing it.

The conversation touches on international strategies as well, including retirement planning that considers geographic arbitrage—how living in countries like Portugal could save hundreds of thousands of dollars over a retirement lifetime while providing a higher quality of life.

Your Permission Slip to Financial Freedom

Perhaps the most important takeaway from this episode is the permission it gives you to approach money with what Melissa calls a "beginner's mind." You don't have to have all the answers right now. You don't have to understand every financial concept perfectly before you start. What you need is the willingness to be coachable and the recognition that today is the best day to start planning for tomorrow.

Melissa's approach is refreshingly practical and shame-free. She acknowledges that many people have complicated relationships with money—whether from childhood conditioning, religious teachings, or societal messaging. But she also provides a clear path forward: education, strategy, and action.

This episode isn't just about money—it's about freedom, choice, and the power to create the life you truly want. It's about recognizing that financial literacy isn't a luxury or something you'll get to eventually. It's a necessity for every woman who wants to live fully, love deeply, and leave a lasting impact on the world.

The time for financial passivity is over. The time for taking control of your financial future is now. As Melissa reminds us, money is simply a tool—but in the right hands, with the right knowledge, it becomes a tool for transformation, not just for yourself, but for generations to come.

quiz.title: What's Your Money Freedom Archetype?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally approach wealth and financial independence. Your mindset shapes your money story—let's uncover yours.

questions.question: When you think about your financial future, what drives you most?

options.text: The feeling that there are opportunities I haven't discovered yet
Making sure I'm protected from worst-case scenarios
Creating enough wealth to live completely on my terms
Building a solid foundation I can count on long-term

questions.question: How do you typically react when someone shares an unconventional money strategy?

options.text: I'm intrigued and want to understand the principles behind it
I'm skeptical until I can thoroughly research the risks
I'm excited if it could accelerate my wealth-building goals
I want to see the long-term track record before considering it

questions.question: What's your biggest frustration with traditional financial advice?

options.text: It feels too generic and doesn't address my unique situation
It often involves more risk than I'm comfortable with
It's too conservative and slow for my timeline
It's not specific enough about the step-by-step process

questions.question: When facing a major financial decision, you tend to:

options.text: Trust your gut feeling after gathering some key information
Research extensively and consult multiple trusted sources
Focus on the potential upside and move quickly if it aligns with your goals
Create a detailed analysis of pros, cons, and long-term implications

questions.question: How do you feel about the statement 'money is freedom'?

options.text: It resonates deeply—I've always sensed money was about more than just security
I agree, but I worry about the risks involved in pursuing wealth
Absolutely—that's exactly why I'm focused on building significant wealth
I agree, and I want a clear plan to systematically build that freedom

questions.question: What would motivate you most to learn advanced wealth strategies?

options.text: Discovering approaches that feel aligned with your values and intuition
Finding strategies that offer growth while protecting your downside
Learning tactics that successful people use to accelerate wealth building
Understanding proven systems you can implement consistently over time

results.title: The Intuitive Builder
results.description: You have a natural sense that there's more to money than what you've been taught, and you're ready to explore beyond traditional advice. You trust your instincts and aren't afraid to question conventional wisdom when it doesn't align with your values. Your openness to new perspectives is your superpower—you just need the right strategies to match your intuitive approach. This episode will give you the advanced wealth-building tools that feel as sophisticated as your instincts.

results.title: The Security Seeker
results.description: You value stability above all else and prefer to make careful, well-researched financial decisions. Your cautious approach has likely served you well, but you may be missing opportunities for growth by playing it too safe. Your methodical nature is actually perfect for implementing the systematic wealth strategies Melissa shares. This episode will show you how to build security AND growth using proven strategies that align with your careful approach.

results.title: The Ambitious Optimist
results.description: You're naturally drawn to big goals and aren't afraid to take calculated risks to achieve them. Your confidence is inspiring, and you intuitively understand that money is a tool for creating the life you want. You're ready to level up but need the sophisticated strategies to match your ambitious vision. This episode will give you the billionaire-level tactics and mindset shifts to accelerate your wealth-building journey.

results.title: The Steady Strategist
results.description: You believe in consistent, disciplined approaches to building wealth over time. You're practical and realistic about money, preferring proven methods to flashy trends. Your steady nature is actually ideal for the long-term wealth strategies that create lasting financial freedom. This episode will show you how to optimize your systematic approach with advanced techniques that compound your steady efforts into significant wealth.

transcript: [music] Hi everyone and welcome back to the manager, the podcast where we reframe the invisible work of women as powerful leadership. I'm your host Amy Rickabus, author of The Manager, entrepreneur, and mama of six. Today's guest is someone who's helping women reclaim their financial power, not through hustle, but through education and strategy. Melissa Northway is a financial educator and wealth strategist with True Path Financial and the former president of Nabo California, where she's been a champion for women in business and leadership. Melissa's mission is simple but radical to empower women with money to understand it, talk about it, and use it as a tool for freedom. She helps individuals, families, and her firm even helps professional athletes build financial security through smart tax strategies, charitable giving, and innovative tools like cash value life insurance and asset swaps that protect wealth while it grows. In this episode, we're talking about how to change your relationship with money, how to protect what you've built, and how to create real financial freedom. Not someday, but starting right now. Welcome to the show, Melissa. I'm so glad you're here. >> Thank you so much. I'm so excited. And thank you. That was a great intro. I was like, who was that lady you're talking about? >> That lady is you. You take that and you grow. Yeah, you own that. >> So, Melissa, you've said that money equals freedom. What do you mean by that? And how has your own relationship with money evolved over time? It's interesting because I'll do webinars or, you know, presentations and I always ask the women in the audience, you know, what is their relationship with money or what's the first word that comes to mind, you know, when when I say the word money. And I just find that so often women are afraid of it. They don't feel like they're, you know, wise enough to be investing. And so I see myself in some of those women because I think of myself when I was younger and I don't think I had the respect that I have now for money and what money represents. And for me, you know, I'll ask, you know, I'll ask women one, what emotion or thought or word comes to mind when I say money, I hear all these interesting, you know, responses. And then the other one is, you know, um, how does it make you feel? And so my relationship has changed over time with my, you know, knowledge and I've I really feel like in the last 6 months I've gotten a master class in finances and wealth building with the firm True Path. You know, it's predominantly I'm pretty much the only woman at the firm. And it's just been such a wonderful experience because I want to now bring this information and knowledge to other women and families and business owners, you know, to help empower them. So I think money is freedom. Freedom to live the life that you want to live. Yeah. >> Without having to worry about money. >> Yeah. Absolutely. Absolutely. >> Yeah. >> As a former president of Navo California, you've been such a force for women in business. What did you notice about how women talk or don't talk about money at that level? >> Gosh, I almost feel at most levels, right? From the business owners to, you know, everyday moms, which we both know is the hardest job on the planet. >> Yes. >> To just, you know, everyday friends. It's not something that women talk about. And I I feel like, you know, I want to shout from the rooftops like you want to talk about money. You want to talk about what do you want for your own life? You know, how is money going to help you achieve your goals? And so I just feel like, you know, it's something that women need to be having these conversations because statistics, you know, the majority of the elderly poor are women, >> you know, and so often women for whatever reason, their family dynamics, cultural, you know, dynamics, they leave the planning to the to their men or partners. >> Yeah. >> And I just want to say, hey, listen, you need to be vested. You need to be you need to know where things are. You need to make sure maybe your partner or husband needs to have help, right? And he's trying to be macho and he he's like, "Well, I got it." Well, maybe he doesn't have it, >> right? >> You know, and maybe he needs, you know, some support. >> Yeah. >> But I think, you know, women need to really be a lot more mindful on having this conversation. Like you and I when we first met, we were like on it. We're so driven by that. We're so excited about it. And it's not because of what it buys so much as what it can do for somebody in your life and your family, but also your community. >> Absolutely. Because the more you make, the more you can help your community, you know, empower. Yeah. The impact. Exactly. So, I think it's important. We need to have these conversations. Men do it on the golf course all the time. >> They sure do. I know. They sit around and talk about financial planning and tax strategies. And I would love it if we could change the culture of women where we could have those same. I I really do. With my best friend, we do talk about these kinds of things. >> I love Yeah. >> Um but she's like one of the only women that I can talk money with. >> Yes. And I think that, you know, I'd love it if talking money became more of a thing that women could could have in their conversation, you know, in our within our wheelhouse, feeling comfortable talking about who we're using for financial planning and 401ks and self-directed Roth IRAs and all of these cool things that we should really kind of know about because if you're making money, you might as well be saving money. >> Well, saving money and making sure that your money is working for you, right? and and and getting an understanding and so you know I would just recommend to your viewers your audience like you know really dive deep what is your relationship with money you know and if if money makes you anxious or you know you just feel like you don't know enough like dive deep into that sit with that and then it's like okay well what do I want at the end game so I'm a little different like I'll I'll say okay what's the endg game for you what do you want retirement to look like how much money do you want to have come through that for and then are we on track for hitting those numbers, you know, and and so, you know, look at it less as something that is burdensome or or scary and more like, hey, I want these things in my life, so I'm going to make and do whatever I have to do to make it happen. >> Yeah. >> But why do you think, Amy, why do you think women don't have these conversations? Because I've actually had women tell me and got and and were upset with me. They're like, "Well, I don't really want to talk about." And I was like, "Oh, geez. Well, I sure do. What time is it?" >> I know. Well, I think that there's a cultural I don't think our mothers generally talked about money. So, we learn a lot of our behavior and conversation or social skills from our mothers who learned them from their mothers. They're going to need to know how to talk tax deferment and they're going to need to know how to invest their money wisely in real estate and other vehicles. She was really ahead of her time. Honestly, she really was. >> And who taught her? Was she selftaught? >> She was selftaught. I think she started at the Wilshshire Country Club as like a secretary and basically was just sharp as a whip and just worked her way up all the way up to the top person in accounting which is the controller >> and she you know just smart as a whip and so good with money and just I really honestly I want to be like Jean when I grow up. I do. She's just amazing. Yeah. >> But how lucky exactly how lucky you are that she came into your life and I know you gave a lot to her. It was a beautiful mutual relationship, you know, um, friendship just from what you had shared with me. But I I think that, you know, I hear so often people, men and women are like, and these are people, high earners, they're like, "Nobody taught me about this or how come more people don't know about this?" And so, it really is, I feel like, a lifelong journey. >> Yeah. >> Of learning about money and and again, understand your relationship with it. And then and then surround yourself with people that you trust. >> Yes. and make it um your mission to like you said, if you don't control it, it's going to control Yeah. >> how how you live your life and and how you end up. >> Yeah, absolutely. Who wants to end up, you know, homeless and be a statistic just because you didn't tend your garden? you know, life is I'm realizing as I'm writing my next book that, you know, like there's all of these things that we have to tend in life and money happens to be one of the fields that we need to be tending and that we need to be okay with the fact that that does need tending because at some point we're going to stop working and we want it maybe we want to have impact or maybe we just want to have time with our family so we can be grandma and be comfortable enough to be a really awesome grandma that can pick our grandkids up from school and maybe teach them, you know. I don't know. My kids are like, "Will you teach my kids? Will you be their homeschool teacher when when I when we have kids?" And I'm like, "Yeah, I want to do that. That's what I want to do when I grow up." >> You Yeah, that's right. >> Yeah. I'd love to do that when I grow up, you know? So, I mean, I think of, you know, where we want to be and all the things, all of the fields we need to tend to get to where we want to end up. >> Right. >> Right. And money being a very important field. >> It is. I mean, it it has the potential to have a lot of impact. >> Yeah. And I I'm always surprised. I've I have heard this in my line of work that they feel that money is evil. And I was like, >> "Wow, how sad to have that obviously that noise came from somewhere, >> but to think that money, you know, of course in the wrong hands or not doing good, but it has the potential to do so so much good." >> It really does, >> you know, and I look at it that way in a positive light. >> I do too. Um, >> I really do cuz I see in fact the thing I think the thing that led to the success that we've really experienced in the last 5 years in the businesses was >> me deciding that I wanted to have a big impact in the nonprofit world. I wanted to have I wanted my goal was I want to donate $100,000 a year to nonprofits. And maybe it's about more, you know. >> Yeah. And whatever level of impact that is, you know, I mean, I think women are very hard on themselves >> about so many things, right? But we get a lot of weird messaging in our society, right? like you can't be too successful, you can't be too or just too much, right? So much of >> that, you know, if you do want to make an impact from here to here, whatever, >> take like you said, you took those steps to get to that point where you can have this kind of impact and I think once you visualize that and I'm such a big believer of you've got to visualize that and then it's going to happen, but you've got to see yourself there. >> Totally. and get down to the granular details of really where you want to be. When I get really honest about this is, you know, I'll be like, "Oh, I want to do this." And it's like, "Okay, Amy, but in order to do this, let's get really specific. Well, I feel like the universe aligns. >> You meet the right people. Well, you know, even like today I met that lady who, you know, she works with women uh venture capitalists who only invest in women IT companies and here we are launching, you know, so the universe will definitely, you know, and and coming from a pure heart. Like, his car is the exact thing that was on our vision board. like my car is the exact thing, you know. Yeah. Exactly. I was like, how dumb is that? Right. It's like you always talk to both, you know. Yeah. I was like, "Let me tell you one thing that has not happened." >> Yeah. [laughter] >> So, needless to say, you didn't work with them. >> I You know, you want to feel like you're on a on an equal footing with someone that there's a mutual respect for one another. >> That's when you work with people. >> Yes. Yes. That's a biggie. >> Yeah. It's a big one. It's a big one for me. It's always a big one for me. I'm not going to work with somebody who can't have mutual respect. >> Yeah. >> Absolutely. >> Yeah. And I think that's beautiful. So, let's talk about cash value life insurance. >> Yeah. I love to talk about called a tier one asset. What does it mean and how can women use it to create long-term financial stability? >> So, a tier one asset is um an asset that is, you know, like a treasury bond, CDs. Banks have to have a certain percentage of their assets on their books need to be tier one, which is considered very safe. And that all happened after, you know, 2008. They need to have a certain proportion. So, cash value life insurance is considered a tier one asset. And it's because you you don't have the risk of of losing money. We have what's called a zero floor. You are mirroring. Let's say for example, we're mirroring the S&P.
